source: WSET L3 Specification + marking key analysis

exam_preparation_guidance.understanding_mark_allocation: Every mark corresponds to one correct fact, observation, or causal link. The mark scheme is additive, not holistic. Length does not earn marks; correctness does.
exam_preparation_guidance.using_command_verbs: Read the command verb before answering. 'Describe' = list features. 'Explain' = give causes. 'Compare' = link two items on shared dimensions. Misreading the verb is the single largest source of avoidable mark loss.
exam_preparation_guidance.SAT_exam_approach: For Unit 2, use the full SAT structure every time, even for simple wines. The marking grid rewards completeness. Skipping a section loses available marks even if your other observations are excellent.

topic_priority_by_RA.RA2: Highest short-answer mark allocation (70 pts). Covers viticulture, vinification, wine styles, storage, service. Prioritise this area.
topic_priority_by_RA.RA3_RA4: 20 pts combined. Covers major regions (France, rest of world). Focus on climate, key grapes, and appellation rules.
topic_priority_by_RA.RA5: 10 pts. Covers sparkling, sweet, and fortified. Know the production method differences (traditional vs. tank vs. transfer).

tasting_preparation_guidance.vocabulary_practice: Practise matching WSET Lexicon descriptors to wine types. Primary aromas must be wine-type appropriate (red wine does not have 'citrus' as a primary descriptor).
tasting_preparation_guidance.structure_recognition: Identify tannin and acidity as structural indicators, not flavour descriptors. High acidity = refreshing/crisp; high tannin = grippy/drying.
tasting_preparation_guidance.quality_calibration: Outstanding wines have complexity (all three aroma categories), balance, length, and clear development. Practise articulating why — not just labelling.
